posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Reports are starting to get repetitive. For the most part, you know who will be at the top. Not saying that a bad thing at all. Those producers are great and they deserve the accolades.

I certainly understand why you feel that way! I do think that I’ve “shuffled the deck” a bit (look where Lachaux and Duroché rank in the report, or Lamy, Henri Germain and Thomas Bouley from the Côte de Beaune), but any vintage report of two very good vintages that features the likes of Leroy, DRC and Coche is certainly going to look a bit iterative if you sort by score—unless something somewhere went badly wrong, which in 2019 and 2020 it happily didn’t. I hope there is added value, at least, in the producer profiles, which often go into quite a bit of detail about winemaking choices and evolutions at each domaine.

But while there are a couple of more under-the-radar names in the report (I would flag up Didier Fornerol and Michel Mallard in particular), that is something I try to do much more of in my annual Spring report, where I look at less famous appellations and less famous winemakers. I have spent a lot of time in the Chalonniase; last year I did a lot of Hautes-Côtes stuff; and this year I’m thinking of digging around a bit more in Fixin and Marsannay. I found that reviewing producers like this in the big end-of-year report was a bit of a waste of time, as everyone scrolls to the top and they don’t really get much exposure. I also don’t see the interest in reviewing producers whose wines are generally not sold as futures from barrel, so in Spring I taste from bottle.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Does anybody know why William stopped reviewing the Lamarche wines, after the 2016 vintage, or perhaps William may be able to respond? He described Nicole Lamarche as one of his favourite winemakers, at the time, so just seems a bit odd that her wines are no longer being reviewed. I would have liked to see his views for this vintage, although other reviews that I have seen, have been favourable.

I certainly didn’t describe her as one of my favorite winemakers!

To be frank, despite talk of the wines getting better, I continue to think that they fall far short of the quality level to which they should aspire.

My comments at the time were:

Having heard much about alleged improvements at Domaine Lamarche, my visit here to taste the bottled 2015s left me disappointed. The domaine’s roster of appellations is formidable, and Nicole Lamarche declares that her aim is to capture elegance, freshness and fruit—an ambition that, in its own terms, I warmly applaud and would be delighted to champion. Elegance, however, is in the eye of the beholder, and I found these wines attenuated, diffuse and drying.

I have largely refrained from entering into some of the discussions about critics feeling compelled to temper their criticism due to the implicit threat of loss of access, but this is a good example of why I find those arguments a bit exaggerated. If I don’t like the wines, why would I care if I’m banned?

Incidentally, since we are going on tangents, a lot of the debate about wine critics’ ethics remind me of (I think) Mark Twain’s line, when asked if he had ever bribed the press for good reviews, to the effect that, given what the English journalist will do for free, he had never found it necessary.

In Burgundy and among the grower champagnes, there are hundreds of small, often family-owned, farms and wineries where attention to the details William frequently highlights can pay big and unexpected dividends. In Bordeaux, by contrast, we focus almost exclusively on the top 60-80 wines, which are produced in the main by very rich organizations using the finest of agricultural techniques available to grow the grapes and the finest available techniques in the winery to produce the wines. Yes, there are hundreds of smaller producers in Bordeaux where William’s approach can reveal unheralded gems; I just think almost no one will care outside of visitors to French supermarkets.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
FWIW (which may be very little), I think his focus on vineyards and the path from vine to bottle will prove to be less relevant in Bordeaux than in Burgundy. In Burgundy and among the grower champagnes, there are hundreds of small, often family-owned, farms and wineries where attention to the details William frequently highlights can pay big and unexpected dividends. In Bordeaux, by contrast, we focus almost exclusively on the top 60-80 wines, which are produced in the main by very rich organizations using the finest of agricultural techniques available to grow the grapes and the finest available techniques in the winery to produce the wines. Yes, there are hundreds of smaller producers in Bordeaux where William’s approach can reveal unheralded gems; I just think almost no one will care outside of visitors to French supermarkets.

We will see! There are second-growths that are machine harvested, however, so best practice is less universal than you might imagine. I also think a more nuanced vision of winemaking can get us beyond some of the false oppositions between ripe fruit and hygienic winemaking on the one hand, and classical balance and aging potential on the other. Bordeaux produces about as much wine as the whole of Australia, yet it is for the most part covered by the wine press in 10-15 days per year, focussed on massive tastings in offices in the city. In this respect, a lot of the complaints that people have about the critical coverage of this region are, in my view, inextricable from the way in which the wines are tasted. But honestly, it is a big project to do things differently, so I am all in favor of encouraging only modest expectations!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Which leads me to more questions; some of which I don’t expect answers. The report seems to indicate that all Burgundy is good, or even very good. 2020 seems like a solid vintage, but really? Every producer is awesome? What about producers not in the report? Some, I expect, didn’t get in for logistical reasons. But some maybe just aren’t that good. If Wikipedia is right and there are some 1100+ Domaines in the Côte d’Or, why choose to write about this particular 100? Hmm. Are there top names that get excluded because the wines just aren’t up to snuff? We don’t hear about those - wine writers are a very discreet bunch.

Well, consider the amount of time it takes to visit 100 domaines and write up the ensuing tasting notes. Obviously, that simply isn’t possible. So there has to be a selection. In that regard, I divide our coverage of the Côte d’Or up into two big reports. The end-of-year report focusses on barrel tastings of wines that are sold as futures, so mainly the more prominent villages of the Côte d’Or (though increasingly domaines are moving away from EP sales and showing finished wines in bottle, which I much prefer). At the same time, I try to look at a good sample of the most recently bottled vintage to see how that has turned out. Then, in Spring, I cover less well known producers, often from less famous villages, along with the Côte Chalonnaise and Hautes-Côtes. This is more of a “discovery” report, ranging widely to find interesting new producers.

Within these two reports, the selection is necessarily, in a sense, arbitrary, in that it’s entirely at my discretion and reflects on the one hand, what interests me and appeals to my palate, and on the other, a desire to offer a broad overview of the vintage along the Côte d’Or as interpreted by producers operating in different stylistic registers. I do dare to break with some of my colleagues/competitors in not always visiting all the producers that received opinion considers “reference points”, because I try to prioritize discovering new estates, and visiting the exact same cast of producers every year, without any time to discover new estates, would be tedious. And yes, I am definitely trying to find good wine, not bad, for our readers; and so the reports are heavily skewed towards the best, not the worst, that I think the region has to offer. If you would like a selection of utterly revolting wines, I could supply one, and sometimes just to remind myself of what the average uninterested consider will encounter, I buy a few bottles of “Bourgogne Rouge” at a local supermarket. It is not the stuff that dreams are made of.

Then, beyond then, there are logistical accidents: a producer forgets an appointment, or gets sick, or their neighbor’s tractor breaks and they have to treat their vineyards for them, or whatever, that normally eliminate a small handful of domaines that I intended to visit from any given report.

posts.name: Troy Stark
posts.cooked:
If you would like a selection of utterly revolting wines, I could supply one, and sometimes just to remind myself of what the average uninterested consider will encounter, I buy a few bottles of “Bourgogne Rouge” at a local supermarket. It is not the stuff that dreams are made of.

Two thoughts:

  1. I would find this report of utterly revolting wine hilariously awesome. I know it will never be done, but oh boy would that be amazing.

  2. Your comment about buying “Bourgogne Rouge” at the supermarket is interesting. We typically vet producers by tasting their Bourgogne rouge (and blanc). If we don’t like it, the assumption is we probably won’t like their more expensive wines. Given the challenges inherent in the making of “good” Bourgogne rouge, and trying to give producers a leg up in this challenge, we stick to more solar vintages when conducting these experiments, understanding that they may give a better indication of the ripeness that will likely bless the mid and upper slopes in “normal” years.

Having laid out the parameters of how we vet producers, and seeing your comment above, I wonder if this is even a helpful exercise? Maybe we are missing out on some great producers who don’t have good plots from which to make a regional wine? Maybe the Bourgogne level is just not a good litmus test for a producer? For example, our first experience with Lafarge was his 2015 rouge, which we decided was too rustic for us. Thus, we have refrained from going any deeper into his stable, even though others seem to love his wines. On the other hand, our first experience with Leroy was a bottle of 2000 Blanc that had been stored on the racks of a retailer for about 16 years without any climate control other than AC. It was incredible.

Also, we find much more variability in rouge than blanc. Is that your experience, too?

Strawman alert!

As with any report in part, we the readers have to address the meaning of omissions. For instance, William has never covered Domaine Dublere, a winery I believe you like quite a bit. Is the reason because he’s just never gotten there or because he doesn’t think their wine is good? In the abstract, either reason is as likely as the other.

To Rich’s larger point about the quality of the 2020s, if we see a report on what may be the best 10% of producers, what does that say about the vintage as a whole? Is it an irregular vintage where only the good producers did well or only certain appellations succeeded and the rest poorly or a vintage consistent across (quality) producers and/or appellations where we can buy with confidence? We can only guess, and unfortunately, William’s introduction did not directly address that point, although given the scores, it appears quality is not at all limited to certain favored vineyards. Perhaps he hasn’t tasted widely enough yet to make that kind of determination, which is fair. This is only the first of several reports he will make.

I do wonder if Rich’s comments was prompted in part by the thread on Galloni’s recent report on 2019 Napa cabs, where AG included some quite low-scoring reviews and some openly scornful notes. Some people have taken those reviews as a sign that we can trust Galloni more because he will include the bad with the good, which may make the good more credible.

Well, consider the amount of time it takes to visit 100 domaines and write up the ensuing tasting notes. Obviously, that simply isn’t possible. So there has to be a selection. In that regard, I divide our coverage of the Côte d’Or up into two big reports. The end-of-year report focusses on barrel tastings of wines that are sold as futures, so mainly the more prominent villages of the Côte d’Or (though increasingly domaines are moving away from EP sales and showing finished wines in bottle, which I much prefer).

Why don’t prominent critics push back against barrel tasting for Burgundy? Just because it’s a useful exercise in Bordeaux (i.e. highly correlated to future assessments of bottled wines) doesn’t make it so in Burgundy. How correlated are they? Is there any reasonable alternative that would still get reviews to consumers when the wines are still available?

Then, in Spring, I cover less well known producers, often from less famous villages, along with the Côte Chalonnaise and Hautes-Côtes. This is more of a “discovery” report, ranging widely to find interesting new producers.

This is why I’m considering subscribing to TWA again, even though pricing and actuarial tables have almost eliminated my purchasing from the region. I’m already not buying/drinking “great” burgundy (unless I already own it), but it would scratch my discovery itch.
Regards,
Peter

I have traumatic memories of a protracted tasting at Chanzy in Bouzeron, who abuse fine appellations all over Burgundy. There are also several large houses with international distribution whose wines should be a lot better than they are. To me, making an atrocious Bourgogne is probably a more venial sin than making a mediocre grand cru, and there are plenty of people who do that. I think almost all the bad wine in Burgundy is explicable by people not having understood the reality of the current market; which is that if you aspire to make great wine, the market will follow you, no matter where you are. Never have producers had more tools at their disposal to reach potential consumers and to explain what they are trying to do to a broad audience, and never has that audience been so receptive. Yet there remain plenty of producers who shoot for volume across the board, and even more who don’t invest enough effort in their lesser AOCs, because “c’est Bourgogne quand même” (“it’s just Bourgone, all the same”).

It’s a direct question, so I will answer candidly. I was very impressed by the wines when I was a student a decade ago, and I even wanted to work the harvest with Louis-Michel one year (the birth of my first child intervened). Since then, I confess I’ve fallen out of love with the wines. The combination of Séguin Moreau/Plantagenet barrel mocha and very minimal extraction make for wines that are very flashy young, but I do wonder how well they will age. Interestingly, I actually tend to think some of Louis-Michel’s earlier vintages, e.g. 2006 (his 2006 Echézeaux is brilliant!) have aged better than some more recent years (e.g. 2012, 2013). But in short, though there is no question that the (enviable) vineyards are very well farmed and that no expense has been spared in terms of materiel, and that in terms of winemaking they are, in a sense, flawless, they are not wines that do very much for me these days.

One quick question about extraction that I find interesting - you talk about minimal extraction with their wines which, anecdotally, it seems more and more producers are aiming for with pneumatic presses, no pigeage, and other “infusion”-oriented practices - do you find that with the riper fruit that most domaines are harvesting in this era that there’s been a trade-off in the stuffing or beneficial/flavour extracts that make it into the wine (and their effect on possible longevity or aging curves)? I get the sense from the reading between the lines of some producer breakdowns that you are finding a bit more of that with some whites but I’m also interested in its effects on red wines. This is quite a broad question, I understand, so apologies in advance.

It’s a huge question! For reds, everyone focusses on punch downs, but no one talks about enzymes, heated post-fermentation macerations, or pressing, all three of which are, at least in my experience, much more extractive than a classic punch down by foot (which is different from a mechanical punchdown). If you do zero punch downs, and then press without tasing the press wine, you can easily make a much more extracted wine than if you do lots of punchdowns and just keep free-run juice. I remember how terrified I was to “over-extract” when I made my first cuvée in Burgundy, and it took me a few years to realize that, if you have great fruit, don’t heat your tanks, don’t add enzymes, and then use a very gentle basket press, you don’t make wines that are chewy or blocky or opaque. I meet producers who tell me they did two punch downs, whose wines are chewy and austere, and then a cuvée that a punched down twice a day for ten days tastes melting and velvety and supple. Go figure.

As for longevity as a correlate of extraction, well, clearly the two are linked. But equally, in Burgundy we are looking at very low pH wines (relative to Bordeaux, Rhône or most New World reds) that can age on their balance and acid, like white wines, much better than you might think. There have also long been debates about this, going back to the early- mid-19th century, when people first started extending macerations beyond 24-48 hours. I tend to think it’s possible to make very delicately extracted reds which will age a long time, but then you have to be very careful about giving them a very oxidative élevage (e.g. lots of new oak plus not much lees).

posts.name: Brian Befano
posts.cooked:
… I tend to think that the sort of generalizations you seem to be looking for in a vintage report are largely meaningless. In a region with such a diversity of sites and producer approaches as Burgundy, vintage charts have always been next to useless. There is no one “definitive” 2020. And there is no way to anticipate whether someone in Burgundy made good wines without tasting them first. I would never recommend anyone to buy by vintage, indiscriminately, “with confidence”.

Edit: I would add that I don’t mean to seem defensive. I simply have serious reservations about generalizing about wines I haven’t tasted; and I prefer to prioritize trying to find good wines rather than bad in my reporting, which I think makes sense. This likely means that there are train wrecks in 2020 that I didn’t taste, along with, I’m sure, some exceptional wines. You will, however, find some critical reviews and commentaries of what I consider less successful 2020s in my report, even if the vast majority of the 100 or so estates I visited did very well indeed.

So refreshing to hear. I appreciate the vintage reports that explain broadly what happened, general challenges, and give insight into trends. I find your reports do this as do Erin’s for Oregon.

Do you post reviews of all the wines you taste or do you hold back some of the negative reviews?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Interesting to note about the point on extraction because of higher temperatures - I believe Jadot is one of the few that still sticks to this, just wondering if any one else still sticks to this practice?

There is a big difference between fermenting hot, which happens naturally (a destemmed two- or three-ton ferment in a wooden tank will generally naturally get up to over 30 degrees centigrade unless you cool it down), and a heated post fermentation maceration whereby you use temperature control to maintain a high temperature. You naturally get a bit of that if you have a tank with good thermal inertia and a decent mass of ferment, but when you start playing around with warming things up at the end, I have frequently seen people go too far. At that stage you have >12% alcohol at play which combined with high temperature and fully broken up berries is really a very extractive environment.

Jadot I think just ferment quite hot but AFAIK they are not heating at the end.

For anyone who’s looking for Fornerol, Lopa sent out an offer on the 2016s and 2019s last night at very sharp pricing. Not sure if there will be any left for the stragglers, but can’t hurt to ask!

And its a very very great Village from Corgoloin. 40 years vines

Glad it delivered, Claus!

I will do a vertical with Didier later this year, because the aging potential is also quite considerable.
